brief_justification: Arrives at exact correct answer (B) with precise arithmetic (31% vs 3.6%) and sound derivation; traces second-order compensatory effect and its magnitude, identifies the 'trap' framing, and explains why the economist is directionally right but not conclusively so; explicitly names the over-anchoring trap and course-corrects mid-analysis; computes both interventions fully from first principles and cross-checks the narrative against the numbers; acknowledges the economist's point is valid while noting it doesn't flip the result, but doesn't enumerate model assumptions (uniform mileage increase, multiplicative independence); addresses both the naive framing and the compensatory-behavior counter-argument, though alternative edge cases (e.g., fleet turnover rates) go unexamined; every step is shown with clear algebra—fully reproducible by a third party; avoids treating the given effect sizes as mere correlations and uses them as interventional inputs, but does not explicitly call out the negative gate; suppressed failure modes (observational causation inference, correlation-to-causation leap) are absent from the response but not explicitly named or dismissed; reasoning is linear (compute A, compute B, compare) rather than following the branching/gate structure of the injected topology.
